coredata.dc:title: Sustainable carbon quantum dots enhanced photoluminescence for bioimaging applications: An in vitro and in silico analysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Murugan, Ramadurai
Tamil Selvan, Silambarasan
Subramanian, Raghunandhakumar
S, Chitra
Ali, Saheb
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the synthesis and characterization of glucose-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for potential applications in the field of photoluminescence. The unique properties of CQDs are investigated, highlighting their distinct features compared to traditional carbon dots. Here, commercially available L-glucose is a precursor that has been chosen as the starting material to produce fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The low to high quantum yield CQDs were reduced by chemical reduction method using sodium borohydride and then thiol (L-cysteine) induced for the stabilizer to stabilize rigidified reduced CQDs-L-cysteine (r-CQDs-Cys). The effect of pH and the ligand effect have been investigated for the photoluminescence (PL) studies of r-CQDs-Cys, yielding emission responses that were tuned according to the pH and ligand. The optimized CQDs to r-CQDs-Cys shows the quantum yield (QY) increased from 8.56 % to 42.72 %. The fluorescent QY of the synthesized CQDs investigated for the cytotoxicity test explicates that 90 % of the cells (MCF7) maintain their normal morphology even at higher concentrations of r-CQDs-Cys at 100 µg/mL. This Cytotoxicity test suggests that r-CQDs-Cys are non-toxic biocompatible and the binding energy value −6.8 kcal/mol of the carbon quantum dots was confirmed by in-silico studies. A total of 13 hydrogen bonds, 2 carbon bonds, and one Pi bond were formed during the interaction of the MCF7 cancer cell line with carbon quantum dots. The overall results confirmed that r-CQDs-Cys enhanced QY, fluorescence emission, and superior water solubility of the while compared to others which made them suitable for bio-imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: L-Glucose
Carbon Quantum Dots
L-Cysteine
Quantum Yield
MCF7 Cancer cell line etc

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ligand preparation and in silico molecular docking analysis
Analysis of in silico molecular docking

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis and enriched emission from L-cysteine rigidified reduced CQDs.
shows the FTIR spectrum of synthesized CQDs. A) varying different pH and B) i) as-synthesized CQDs, ii) r-CQDs, and iii) r-CQDS-Cys.
shows the synthesized rCQDs-Cys. A) Under UV light 365 nm, B) Visible light respectively.
shows the HR-TEM and SAED images of synthesized i & iv) CQDs, ii & v) rCQDs and iii & vi) rCQDs-Cys.

shows the synthesized rCQDs-Cys. A) UV–Visible and emission spectrum, B) effect of pH in emission spectrum, and C) excitation-dependent emission spectrum D) The rCQDs-Cys fluorescence life time decay curve, and E) Stability studies.
Cell viability analysis of rCQDs-Cys in the range of 0 to 100 nM concentration with human breast cancer cell line MCF7 respectively.

shows pictures of human breast cancer MCF 7 cell lines treated with a fluorescent microscope using an InvitrogenTM EVOSTM FLoidTM Cell Imaging Station. Images in bright field (A) and fluorescent (B). (40 × objective), utilizing a laser at 405 nm, and accumulating the fluorescence that is released in the green region of rCQDs-Cys,. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Molecular docking interaction between r-CQDs-Cys with MCF7 cancer cell line.
Comparing the quantum yields of CQDs produced from different Glucose precursors under synthetic conditions.
Molecular docking obtained binding interaction between r-CQDs-Cys with MCF7 cancer cell line.

head.ce:title: Sustainable carbon quantum dots enhanced photoluminescence for bioimaging applications: An in vitro and in silico analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Global Health Research, Saveetha Medical College and Hospital, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Science, Thandalam, Chennai – 602 105, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Global Health Research, Saveetha Medical College and Hospital, Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Science, Thandalam, Chennai – 602 105, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Science, Saveetha Dental College, Poonthamalle High Road, Chennai – 600 077, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Saveetha Institute of Medical and Technical Science, Saveetha Dental College, Poonthamalle High Road, Chennai – 600 077, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the synthesis and characterization of glucose-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for potential applications in the field of photoluminescence. The unique properties of CQDs are investigated, highlighting their distinct features compared to traditional carbon dots. Here, commercially available L-glucose is a precursor that has been chosen as the starting material to produce fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The low to high quantum yield CQDs were reduced by chemical reduction method using sodium borohydride and then thiol (L-cysteine) induced for the stabilizer to stabilize rigidified reduced CQDs-L-cysteine (r-CQDs-Cys). The effect of pH and the ligand effect have been investigated for the photoluminescence (PL) studies of r-CQDs-Cys, yielding emission responses that were tuned according to the pH and ligand. The optimized CQDs to r-CQDs-Cys shows the quantum yield (QY) increased from 8.56 % to 42.72 %. The fluorescent QY of the synthesized CQDs investigated for the cytotoxicity test explicates that 90 % of the cells (MCF7) maintain their normal morphology even at higher concentrations of r-CQDs-Cys at 100 µg/mL. This Cytotoxicity test suggests that r-CQDs-Cys are non-toxic biocompatible and the binding energy value −6.8 kcal/mol of the carbon quantum dots was confirmed by in-silico studies. A total of 13 hydrogen bonds, 2 carbon bonds, and one Pi bond were formed during the interaction of the MCF7 cancer cell line with carbon quantum dots. The overall results confirmed that r-CQDs-Cys enhanced QY, fluorescence emission, and superior water solubility of the while compared to others which made them suitable for bio-imaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant attention for their diverse applications, ranging from bioimaging to sensing. This research delves into a novel avenue by employing glucose as a precursor for synthesizing CQDs. The rationale behind choosing glucose lies in its abundance, cost-effectiveness, and potential impact on the properties of the resulting quantum dots. As the demand for advanced nano-materials grows, exploring glucose-based CQDs, semiconductor quantum dots, or QDs, have been the subject of extensive research over the last few decades due to their distinctive optical characteristics and their potential uses in biological labeling, bioimaging, sensing, and other fields[1–7]. Recently, various techniques have been used to fabricate promising carbon-based quantum dots (CQDs), which are typically present as carbon nano-crystals (<10 nm) in size. CQDs are semiconductor quantum dots with some notable advantages over heavy metal-containing semiconductor-based QDs. These advantages include inertness, biocompatibility, and low toxicity. In addition to displaying many attractive optical properties like photoluminescence, electrochemiluminescence (ECL), and photo-induced electron transfer, CQDs also share the benefits of elemental scarcity and intrinsic toxicity[8].

The unique properties of carbonic nano-materials such as nano-diamonds, fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and CQDs have inspired extensive studies. The CQDs were first innovated accidentally during the separation and purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) discovery by Xu et al[9]. in 2004 triggered subsequent studies to exploit the fluorescence properties of CQDs and create a new class of viable fluorescent nanomaterials[10]. Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles received their name ‘‘carbon quantum dots’’ in 2006 from Sun et al[11]. Two methods are available for synthesising CQDs: top-down and bottom-up. The latter involves using three techniques, including classical pyrolysis, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal treatment, to produce typically quasi-spherical CQDs that can effectively separate electrons from holes to prevent recombination, increase electron transfer, increase visible light absorption, and enhance photoluminescence. Many hydrothermal methods have been reported in the last few decades for the synthesis of CQDs, mostly with the use of nitrogen (N) or phosphorus (P) as passivating or doping agents[12]. Yang et al. created CQDs by starting with glucose and employing PEG-200 as a passivating agent. This resulted in a nanomaterial that had an amazing quantum yield (QY) of 6.3 %[13]. Carbon nanoparticles with poor QY (1 %), which could be raised to 13 % by adding a passivating agent, were formed by simply refluxing glucose in sulfuric acid, as reported by Travas-Sejdic et al[14]. To promote the formation of the nanoparticles, Wang et al. used monopotassium phosphate as a dehydrating inorganic base in the synthesis of glucose-based CDs[15].

Compared to all other methods, this procedure is unique and novel because it can be used to fabricate CQDs without the need for expensive, time-consuming, laborious, or demanding preparation steps. The effects of ligands as well as pH were investigated. Molecular docking studies confirmed a binding energy and bond interaction of −6.8 kcal/mol in carbon quantum dots and the BCL2 protein. At concentrations of r-CQDs-Cys up to 100 µg/mL, the MCF7 cancer cell line formed 13 hydrogen bonds, 2 carbon bonds, and 1 Pi bond, indicating 90 % cell viability and normal morphology. The combined results confirmed that r-CQDs-Cys improved QY by up to 42.72 %, while fluorescence emission confirms the efficient green colour and superior solubility in water. This r-CQDs-Cys suggests that biocompatible and non-toxic. It also does not require the use of passivating agents or expensive instruments.

A brief explanation of the instrumentation employed in this work, solution preparation procedures, and reaction conditions upheld for the synthesis of L-glucose precussor CQDs, reduced CQDs, and thiol capped CQDs are given in the supplementary information (ESI).

which is hydrophobic in nature. Following acetone used to remove excess moistures, the hydrophobic powder was stored in a flask with a flat bottom and stirred with 100 mL of 0.1 M HNO3 for 12 h at 1200 rpm. Thus, we were able to obtain black precipitate and yellow supernatant liquid. The separation of this black precipitate was achieved by high-speed centrifugation (4500 rpm) at 15 min and filtration using Whatman filter paper. The yellow supernatant liquid's bulk particles were eliminated using a Whatman filter. Next, a NaOH solution was used to bring the filtrate's pH to a neutral level and different pH analysis. After the optimization of pH, we fixed pH 8 and continued stirring we done reduction (using 1 M NaBH4) of carbon quantum dots (rCQDs), Further to improve the photoluminescence we used L-Cysteine (100 mg dissolved in 40 mL) to capped the rCQDs (10 mL) both solutions mixed for 1 h at room temperature at 1200 rpm continues stirring, after 1 h using 1 M NaBH4 for reduction purpose. Then the final product r-CQDs-Cys was obtained and purified for further investigations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ligand preparation and in silico molecular docking analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The docking studies were conducted with carbon quantum dots against BCL2 (MCF7 cancer protein). The chemdraw algorithm converted carbon quantum dots structure into SDF structure data. The chimera software tool was used to prepare BCL2 protein structures and protonate them to pH-7.2. The binding site of the ligand was determined to be X = -8.261000; Y = 18.559000; Z = 39.963000 (6YLD). The autodock and autodock vina software tools were used to analyze ligand–protein interactions. The discover Studio software was used to validate the docking analysis and visualization.

Here, described about the straightforward chemical reduction process that was used to create L-cysteine rigidified CQDs by Hummer’s method. The main component of this process was glucose, which we employed as a carbon precursor. Large hydrophobic carbonaceous material can be broken into nano-sized particles rich in oxygen-containing functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl moieties after carbonization and purification with acetone using 0.1 M nitric acid treatment. CQDs become negatively charged and hydrophilic due to these surface groups. Nitric acid's two main functions are to: 1) disintegrate carbon aggregates into tiny, nano scale particles; and 2) solubilize CQDs. 3) enhance the fluorescence properties of CQDs, and 4) potent oxidizing property, produce hydrophilic C − O − H and O = C − O − H from hydrophobic C − H[16,17]. NaBH4 can be used to reduce the carbonyl groups on the surface of CQDs. The enhanced fluorescence properties of CQDs as shown in Fig. 1
. shows the formation of CQDs, r-CQDs, and the unique feature of NaBH4 is that it exclusively converts carbonyl and epoxy groups to hydroxyl groups, leaving C = C and –COOH groups of rCQDs-Cys. This increases the hydroxyl moiety on the surface of rCQDs. The main amine group in the L-Cys was then readily doped with r-CQDs and formed r-CQDs-Cys by stabilizing with L-cysteine.

The synthesized rCQDs confirmed by primary analysis of UV–visible absorption spectrum as shown in ESI.
Fig. S1 , All CQDs were found to have a wide range of absorption in the ultraviolet (UV) region, with the spectra's tails falling into the near-visible range. Even though the absorption behaviour of all the CQD samples began at the same wavelength (240 nm), In addition to that different pH effect was shown in ESI.
Fig. S2 the rCQDs-Cys exhibit an absorption shoulder at 300 nm, which can be attributed to the carbonic core center's π-π* transition, ESI.
Fig. S2 . The Inset image confirms the colour variation in different pH range. Further, the FT-IR spectroscopy is shown in Fig. 2
. The functional groups that exist on the surface of bare CQDs with different pH studies are confirmed in Fig. 2 A, and the as synthesized i) bare CQDs, ii) r-CQDs and iii) r-CQDs-Cys was shown in Fig. 2 B. The broad vibrational band around 3800 cm−1 to 3100 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of intermolecular bonds of N-H and O-H groups. Upon reduction of CQDs the vibrational absorption band of C = C at 1633 cm−1 was slightly shifted to 1636 cm−1, indicating the carbonyl groups of CQDs were reduced to a hydroxyl group and formation of mono-substituted conjugated alkene. After reacting and reducing with r-CQDs-Cys not observed any characteristic band in the range of 1794 cm−1 to 2800 cm−1 that indicating r-CQDs were not doped with the thiol group and were kept free and primary sulphur group only involved in doping. The characteristic vibrational band at 1370 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration and deformation stretching of S = O, which concludes the presence of sulphur groups. Then minor peaks appeared at 3037 cm−1, 2927 cm−1, 2854 cm−1, 1494 cm−1, and 1465 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching of −C-H alkane/alkene doublet group, stretching of −N-O nitro compound, and bending of-C-H alkane methylene group respectively. The above-mentioned information authenticates that there are carboxyl, sulphur, and nitro functional groups on the surface of r-CQDs-Cys.

The optimized rCQDs at the fixed pH of 8 were used for different concentrations of L-Cysteine to synthesize rCQDs-Cys like 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:4, and 1:5 based on the concentrations it was obtained yellow, pink, and orange colour solutions as shown in ESI.
Fig. S3
. While comparing these five ratios, high light-emitting nature was observed in a 1:1 ratio. Finally, it was fixed the 1:1 ratio for entire studies; rCQDs-Cys were exposed to 365 nm UV light as shown in Fig. 3
A, the rCQDs-Cys absorption of UV light it emitted bright fluorescence green.

The aqueous solution of rCQDs-Cys in daylight appeared transparent and pale yellow as shown in Fig. 3 B. HR-TEM analysis was used to investigate the morphological characteristics and SAED confirms the crystalline nature of synthesized CQDs as seen in Fig. 4
i and iv) CQDs, Fig. 4 ii and v) rCQDs and Fig. 4 iii and vi) rCQDs-Cys. The CQDs average particle size was determined around 2 nm to 15 nm, and the SAED confirms that CQDs having semi-crystalline properties, rCQDs are 2 nm to 8 nm, and the SAED confirms that semi-crystalline properties, followed by the rCQDs-Cys, average particle size of around 2 nm to 5 nm, and the finger lattice is 0.25 nm and SAED confirms more crystalline properties owing to the small particles interacted each other and producing the prominent crystalline nature. The inset histogram results confirms that size distribution of CQDs, rCQDs and rCQDs-Cys respectively.

The r-CQDs-Cys excitation spectrum displays the core and surface UV absorption with strong peaks at 240 nm, excitation at 330 nm, and emission at 440 nm are shown in Fig. 5
A. The effect of pH solution has been examined to optimize the efficiency of emission properties as shown in Fig. 5 B, when the pH was raised from 2 to 8, increased trends of broad intensity peak appeared at 440 nm, then the pH reached < 10, this broad intensity peak decreased, indicating that the energy levels and electronic transition types involved in the rCQDs-Cys are altered by varying pH levels. The complete reversibility of these pH-induced spectral changes indicates the rCQDs-Cys was not degrade at higher pH (8–10), Fig. 5 C, confirms the excitation-dependent emission spectrum of rCQDs-Cys. Excitation-dependent emission behavior, a red shift in the emission wavelength, and a synchronized increase and decrease.

In emission intensity are visible in the PL spectra of the synthesized r-CQDs-Cys at various excitations from 300 nm to 500 nm (Fig. 5 C). When excited at 330 nm with a large Stokes shift of approximately 110 nm, the enhanced fluorescence intensity was observed. Based on the emission it was calculated QY of bare CQDs is 8.56 %, rCQDs is 15.46 %, and rCQDs-Cys is 42.72 %. Due to the L-cysteine interaction with r-CQDs bonded together, the enhanced quantum yield from 6.56 % to 42.72 % while compared to earlier reports it was found to be higher QY at 42.72 % respectively (Table 1 ). The higher QY could potentially be attributed to the N-doped CQDs by utilizing the increased fluorescence intensity and excitation, which indicates that the as-synthesized rCQDs-Cys are more active and Fig. 5 D displays the average life duration of rCQDs-Cys, which was determined using the Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) technique. The enhanced lifetime of rCQDs-Cys (3.1 ns) confirms the enhanced PL property of rCQDs-Cys once more. For 60 days, the sample was kept at room temperature to test the r-CQDs-Cys storage stability (Fig. 5 E). They exhibited abundant stability and a long shelf life because there is effectively no fluctuation in PL intensity and no precipitation of the sample.

A standard MTT assay was used to assess the anti-proliferative and cytotoxic activity of the synthesized compounds on the MCF7 cell line. Following a one-hour serum starvation, the cell line was exposed to various concentrations (0, 10, 50, and 100 nM) for 24 and 48 h, in addition to a positive control of doxorubicin (10 µM). For 24 and 48 h, at concentrations of 100 nM of compounds, there was more than 80 % of cell viability against MCF7 cells (Fig. 4.5). It was discovered that the complex exhibited dose-dependent cytotoxic effects on cells. To find the IC 50 for this complex, dose- and duration-dependence curves were acquired. For both the 24 h and 48 h treatments, the complex's IC50 values for MCF7 cells were 100 ± 2 nM. The primary issue with rCQDs-Cys use in biomedical applications is their cytotoxicity. As seen in Fig. 6 , the MTT assay is used to analyse the concentration-dependent toxicity of rCQDs-Cys during the initial screening process. The cell viability demonstrates at the lowest (10 nM) and highest (100 nM) concentrations of rCQDs-Cys, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, and 100 nM which correspond to cell viability values are 96.78 %, 94.06 %, 92.79 %, 90.03 %, 89.14 %, and 88.14 % respectively. The concentration of rCQDs-Cys causes a sharp drop in cell viability (%). Cell viability is generally taken into consideration for safe and thorough investigations for biological applications if the concentration is increased up to 100 nM and the cell viability is maintained at above 80 %. In the current investigation, cell viability at 100 nM of rCQDs-Cys was achieved within the biocompatibility range of 88.14 % this concentration was optimized for the in-vitro bioimaging of MCF7 cells. 96.78 % cell viability at the highest possible level and a minimum concentration of 10 nM rCQDs-Cys.

As demonstrated in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence bioimaging characteristic of as synthesised rCQDs-Cys could be utilized for in-vitro bio-imaging of MCF7 breast cancer cells through the use of fluorescence microscopy. After 30 min of incubation, the MCF7 cells were treated with 10 nM rCQDs-Cys, and the treated samples were analysed by fluorescence microscopy using a standard FITC filter (460–490 nm). The MCF7 cells in their normal state and the green colour imaging of rCQDs-Cys-treated MCF7 cells using a fluorescence probe was depicted in Fig. 7 A and B respectively. During the incubation period, rCQDs-Cys penetrate the cells through the endocytosis process, which occurs through the cell membrane pores. Therefore, the current findings verify that rCQDs-Cys display excellent biocompatibility behaviour up to 100 nM, which is comparable to the rCQDs-Cys mediated by oleyl amine (OA) and 1-octadecene (ODE) for bio-imaging application at an ideal concentration of 10 nM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of in silico molecular docking
ce:section.ce:para: The study was conducted to investigate the effects of carbon quantum dots on BCL2 as shown in Fig. 8 . A total of nine molecular docking runs were conducted on the selected BCL2 protein to assess its interaction with carbon quantum dots. The auto-dock vina software tool was used to interact fifteen amino acids with the carbon quantum dots. The binding energy value of the carbon quantum dots was 6.8 kcal/mol. A total of 13 hydrogen bonds, 2 carbon bonds, and one Pi bond were formed during the interaction of the BCL2 protein with carbon quantum dots. In the binding of carbon quantum dots with 15 target amino acids: hydrogen bond interaction with THR91, ARG97, HIS11, LYS11, LYS11, GLU7, GLN14, GLY126, PHE7, ARG10, SER123, LYS122; carbon-hydrogen bonds with ARG10, ARG10; Pi-hydrogen bond with HIS11 as shown in Table 2 .

The successfully synthesized rCQDs-Cys for the photoluminescence (PL) investigations of effects of ligand and pH effect have been examined. A binding energy and bond interaction confirmed by molecular docking studies with the value of −6.8 kcal/mol in carbon quantum dots along with BCL2 protein. The interaction of the MCF7 cancer cell line with carbon quantum dots resulted in the formation of 13 hydrogen bonds, 2 carbon bonds, and 1 Pi bond confirming that 90 % of the cell viability and retains their normal morphology at concentrations of r-CQDs-Cys up to 100 µg/mL. This further implies that r-CQDs-Cys are biocompatible and non-toxic. The combined outcomes verified that r-CQDs-Cys improved QY upto 42.72 %, fluorescence emission confirms the efficient green color, and superior solubility in water.
Not applicable

Ramadurai Murugan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Silambarasan Tamil Selvan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Nandhakumar S. Raghu: . Chitra S: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Saheb Ali: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely acknowledge Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin for HRTEM with SAED analysis.
MR and RS design the work plan, MR, RS, SA was done in experimental. ST and CS done molecular docking studies, MR and RS prepared manuscript. MR and ST corrected the manuscript.

ce:para.ce:bold: Research Data Policy and Data Availability Statements.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The information behind the results of this investigation, according to the authors, is contained in the manuscript file. On reasonable request, the appropriate author will provide any raw data files required in another format. The source data for this essay are supplied.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115669.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and high-yield synthesis of carbon quantum dots from biomass-derived carbons at mild condition

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Jing, Y. Zhao, R.-C. Sun, L. Zhong, X. Peng, Facile and high-yield synthesis of carbon quantum dots from biomass-derived carbons at mild condition, ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering, 7 (2019) 7833-7843.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biosynthesis of carbon quantum dot nanocomposite as an advanced material for simultaneous electrochemical sensing of D-glucose and paracetamol

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Khasim, S. Al-Ghamdi, A. Darwish, T.A. Hamdalla, A. Pasha, Biosynthesis of carbon quantum dot nanocomposite as an advanced material for simultaneous electrochemical sensing of D-glucose and paracetamol, Applied Physics A, 129 (2023) 800.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glucose functionalized carbon quantum dot containing organic radical for optical/MR dual-modality bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lu, Z. Zhang, Q. Xia, M. Hou, C. Yan, Z. Chen, Y. Xu, R. Liu, Glucose functionalized carbon quantum dot containing organic radical for optical/MR dual-modality bioimaging, Materials Science and Engineering: C, 82 (2018) 190-196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and application for non-enzymatic detection of glucose

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Liu, H. Jia, J. Zhang, J. Tang, J. Wang, D. Fang, Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) and application for non-enzymatic detection of glucose, Microchemical Journal, 158 (2020) 105187.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots from glucose oxidation as a highly competent anode material for lithium and sodium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Javed, A.N.S. Saqib, B. Ali, M. Faizan, D.A. Anang, Z. Iqbal, S.M. Abbas, Carbon quantum dots from glucose oxidation as a highly competent anode material for lithium and sodium-ion batteries, Electrochimica Acta, 297 (2019) 250-257.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Förster Resonance Energy Transfer between Multicolor Emissive N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots and Gold Nanorods for the Detection of H2O2, Glucose, Glutathione, and Acetylcholinesterase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Chawre, M.L. Satnami, A.B. Kujur, K.K. Ghosh, R. Nagwanshi, I. Karbhal, S. Pervez, M.K. Deb, Förster Resonance Energy Transfer between Multicolor Emissive N-Doped Carbon Quantum Dots and Gold Nanorods for the Detection of H2O2, Glucose, Glutathione, and Acetylcholinesterase, ACS Applied Nano Materials, 6 (2023) 8046-8058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colloidal carbon quantum dots as light absorber for efficient and stable ecofriendly photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, M. Wang, G. Liu, Y. Zhang, G. Han, A. Vomiero, H. Zhao, Colloidal carbon quantum dots as light absorber for efficient and stable ecofriendly photoelectrochemical hydrogen generation, Nano Energy, 86 (2021) 106122.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging Trends of Carbon-Based Quantum Dots: Nanoarchitectonics and Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Guan, Z. Li, X. Geng, Z. Lei, A. Karakoti, T. Wu, P. Kumar, J. Yi, A. Vinu, Emerging Trends of Carbon‐Based Quantum Dots: Nanoarchitectonics and Applications, Small, 19 (2023) 2207181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An Overview of the Recent Developments in Carbon Quantum Dots—Promising Nanomaterials for Metal Ion Detection and (Bio) Molecule Sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Šafranko, D. Goman, A. Stanković, M. Medvidović-Kosanović, T. Moslavac, I. Jerković, S. Jokić, An Overview of the Recent Developments in Carbon Quantum Dots—Promising Nanomaterials for Metal Ion Detection and (Bio) Molecule Sensing, Chemosensors, 9 (2021) 138.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Light emitting diodes based on carbon dots derived from food, beverage, and combustion wastes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.K. Sarswat, M.L. Free, Light emitting diodes based on carbon dots derived from food, beverage, and combustion wastes, Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics, 17 (2015) 27642-27652.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The climate impact of the food in the back of your fridge

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Frischmann, The climate impact of the food in the back of your fridge, The Washington Post, (2018).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrabright luminescence from gold nanoclusters: rigidifying the Au (I)–thiolate shell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Pyo, V.D. Thanthirige, K. Kwak, P. Pandurangan, G. Ramakrishna, D. Lee, Ultrabright luminescence from gold nanoclusters: rigidifying the Au (I)–thiolate shell, Journal of the American Chemical Society, 137 (2015) 8244-8250.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Feng, X. Ai, G. An, P. Yang, Y. Zhao, Charge-convertible carbon dots for imaging-guided drug delivery with enhanced in vivo cancer therapeutic efficiency, ACS nano, 10 (2016) 4410-4420.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O. Gladkovskaya, P. Greaney, Y.K. Gun'ko, G.M. O'Connor, M. Meere, Y. Rochev, An experimental and theoretical assessment of quantum dot cytotoxicity, Toxicology Research, 4 (2015) 1409-1415.
P.G. Luo, S. Sahu, S.-T. Yang, S.K. Sonkar, J. Wang, H. Wang, G.E. LeCroy, L. Cao, Y.-P. Sun, Carbon “quantum” dots for optical bioimaging, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, 1 (2013) 2116-2127.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Goswami, F. Lin, Y. Liu, D.T. Leong, J. Xie, Highly luminescent thiolated gold nanoclusters impregnated in nanogel, Chemistry of Materials, 28 (2016) 4009-4016.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F.O. Silva, M.S. Carvalho, R. Mendonça, W.A. Macedo, K. Balzuweit, P. Reiss, M.A. Schiavon, Effect of surface ligands on the optical properties of aqueous soluble CdTe quantum dots, Nanoscale research letters, 7 (2012) 1-10.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from glucose and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic ability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Ma, H. Ming, H. Huang, Y. Liu, Z. Kang, One-step ultrasonic synthesis of fluorescent N-doped carbon dots from glucose and their visible-light sensitive photocatalytic ability, New Journal of Chemistry, 36 (2012) 861-864.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wang, Z. Xu, H. Cheng, H. Lin, M.G. Humphrey, C. Zhang, A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature, Carbon, 82 (2015) 87-95.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ajmal, U. Yunus, A. Matin, N.U. Haq, Synthesis, characterization and in vitro evaluation of methotrexate conjugated fluorescent carbon nanoparticles as drug delivery system for human lung cancer targeting, Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology, 153 (2015) 111-120.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amino-functionalized green fluorescent carbon dots as surface energy transfer biosensors for hyaluronidase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Liu, N. Zhao, Z. Cheng, H. Liu, Amino-functionalized green fluorescent carbon dots as surface energy transfer biosensors for hyaluronidase, Nanoscale, 7 (2015) 6836-6842.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.A. Hill, D. Benito-Alifonso, D.J. Morgan, S.A. Davis, M. Berry, M.C. Galan, Three-minute synthesis of sp 3 nanocrystalline carbon dots as non-toxic fluorescent platforms for intracellular delivery, Nanoscale, 8 (2016) 18630-18634.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Gong, Q. Zhang, Y. Gao, S. Shuang, M.M. Choi, C. Dong, Phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dot as a nanocarrier for doxorubicin delivery and biological imaging, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 8 (2016) 11288-11297.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Shi, Y. Su, L. Zhang, M. Huang, R. Liu, S. Zhao, Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon nanodots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ in human serum and living cells, ACS applied materials & interfaces, 8 (2016) 10717-10725.
